# Transcrição: 2.5-1 Intro to Dynamic Product Ads

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hey, this is D-Pesh and in this video you're going to learn a bit more about dynamic product ads. Dynamic product ads, I think were brought out by Facebook in about 2015 and it enables e-commerce sites in particular, although they can be used for other formats too, to be able to showcase their products directly from their catalogue which is fed into the e-commerce platform, i.e. Shopify or e-commerce or whatever you're using and to be able to feed those products with all the details back into Facebook. So I'm hoping you've done your groundwork on what DPAs are. I'm going to show you a bit more about how you actually manage your catalogue, how you analyse your catalogue and how can you squeeze a bit more value out of them as well. So the catalogue is accessed from your mega menu. Once you're in there, you'll end up on the catalogue landing page which may differ but generally what you see are recommendations, potentially errors and warnings regarding your catalogue. So here for example in this particular catalogue that I'm in, there's a couple of recommendations. There are no collection ads running, collection ads running and the recommendation is to create a collection ad and that's great. But what's actually more interesting is the errors that are highlighted. These errors here are to do with missing data fields but this one in particular is really kind of a handy one to keep an eye on because essentially it gives you an idea of any problems with your catalogue. So the way the dynamic product catalogue works is that the data is fed into Facebook either by your e-commerce platform or it could be a third party platform. You can even use your Google shopping feed and upload that into Facebook or you can actually pass the data dynamically from your website. When it comes to the website itself and I'll just use my general store as an example here. So this is the product page, there's a hands-free kitchen colander and if you look at the pixel will help you here and I expand out view content. So this is a Shopify store. Shopify do this automatically. So in this instance on this particular store I've just gone with the default Shopify link up with Facebook which is to drop your account, your ID into Shopify and it will connect Shopify to Facebook and it's really really handy when you're especially when you're new because actually Shopify and this also applies for other Econ platforms take care of the data that's required to make a dynamic catalogue work. So there's a few fields here to highlight. The value obviously is brought back, the value is minus any kind of currency

## [03:04 - 06:06]

and that's actually passed separately using the three character ISO code for the currency. There's also content category that's passed, this is optional but really the main things you need are the currency, the value and the content IDs and also the content type. So you can have a product group, you can have individual products but the content IDs especially if you've got variants is important here as well but the content ID is what matches it back to your catalogue. So without this content ID your dynamic product catalogue cannot work. So if you're installing it manually you can go through the developer support to figure out which data you need, get your developer to pass it through or you can actually install a third party, plug in and it will take care of it for you. It's always easier to not have to hand code it and then essentially once the data is provided in the format that Facebook needs it's passed as an attribute for your pixel event. So I'm firing the view content here, that's kind of generally the norm for product pages in particular and that's the kind of standard that most e-com sites use but it can apply to any pixel. So for example here if I add to cart you'll be able to see the pixel that's generated on the add to cart. So here again this is a typical action from Shopify. When you add to cart the add to cart event is fired and the same product is sent from view content into add to cart. Now that will go back to Facebook and it will register a view content and an add to cart and it will also register the fact that I've added this particular product to my cart. So when it actually comes to retargeting and you say I want to retarget anyone that's added to cart using a dynamic product add that's the hookup, that's the link that it's using and it will return that add as the primary product in your dynamic product add and then it will figure out other ads to display. So if I've only just viewed this one product and you're running a carousel of dynamic product ads then the first add will generally be this product I viewed and then it might show other related products based on my browser behavior or other customer data if that's not present. So that's the kind of basics of how the dynamic products ads work. Once you've hooked up your catalog so if I just click back here the product data sources defines the catalogs that are linked and you can actually have more than one data source. This could be for example if you want to manage your categories or departments separately or you've got different providers etc etc. There's different ways of running data sources so you can also add them manually you can use data feeds and as I mentioned you can use the Facebook pixel directly. So in this example if I have my data set up correctly like this actually what you

## [06:06 - 09:06]

can do is plug your data directly from your website into Facebook. So Facebook knows this particular URL is related to this product it has these attributes and actually when it goes into filling in your catalog data Facebook uses your site data. The site data has to be set up in the exact way Facebook need it otherwise it doesn't work. The most traditional way of adding the catalog is using data feeds so that's just something on data feeds themselves. So once you've got the data feed set up then it will be available under the catalog campaign objective where you can actually select the catalog and you can just define your audiences and run campaigns from that. I'm not going to cover that off in this particular video but I just want to give you the inner workings of the catalog section right now. So once you've got your feed set up and it's connected you know for example here this feed is from a third party provider called Funneling. There are many others as well and this will drop in every day at 6 a.m. so every day that catalog is updated. Now if you've got a high volume e-commerce site and you know you're making lots and lots of sales a day do you really want to wait 24 hours for your stock availability to update? Maybe not so you'd want to kind of balance the updates based on the amount of activity on your site. This site for example there's two and a half thousand products it sells I don't know 250 maybe 300 products a day it's not going to make a dent on the stock levels anytime soon so once a day it's fine if you're selling thousands thousands maybe even tens of thousands of products a day and your stock levels are always fluctuating then I would encourage you to update that a bit more frequently. So the other part the other really cool part of this is you can see your full product set as well so I'm actually going to use my general store just to show you how the products actually look so this is a store I've set up again another store for testing and for this particular store I'm just importing items into my Shopify store from Aliexpress I'm setting those products up and they get fed automatically to Facebook via Shopify so if I click into a product let's say this product here so the the details here that that's a bug that's something which is a bug on on my product side so let me add a product that has a price bring one out stress-free colander so this relates to the same product that we viewed on the site item group ID ends in 659

you can search for products there's only 132 on this particular store but you can search by different attributes here on the left hand side but what I like to do is to create product sets based on how I'm marketing so for example if I look at the price ranges here and you know there's 999 items do I really want those coming up in re-targeting if I don't then I can actually create a product set based on pricing so if I say the price of the product is minimum 20 dollars and I don't want products below that being shown then I can kind of have a look at that so you go into product sets you create a new product set and you do your search so I'm going to search for items with prices greater than 20 US dollars and it comes up with 96 items so I know now if anything's going to come up in my re-targeting it's going to be an AOV that's going to make it worthwhile for me to market so if you are running dynamic products ads and you've got items which are one two three four five dollars do you really want to re-target your customers with those products probably not because they're most likely add-ons accessories etc so you want to market your highest selling items and for me that's anything above 20 so you can kind of give it a name here items above 20 dollars and that's pretty much it you can add other items in you can add other filters etc etc but for this one if I just create that here now what that means is when I create my ad set I can select which catalog which sub-catalog I'm actually marketing to so this one is items above 20 dollars and I can make sure that for that particular ad set let's say I'm targeting anyone that's viewed content or added to cart in the last I don't know seven days I want to make sure that the products they see are above 20 dollars now why would you perhaps want lower ticket items let's say for example it's post-purchase and you want to give them a promotion seven days after they've actually made their purchase you might put items below 20 dollars and actually just try and bump up the value of that customer chances are because they've just bought they will if it's a good offer they will buy again your cost per acquisition should be super low and therefore selling a lower cost item can still work or you might want to create cross-sell

## [12:11 - 15:14]

catalogs or upsell catalogs there's different ways of slicing and dicing the data when it comes to catalogs so that's the overview of creating product sets and the last thing that I want to actually show you is actually on the diagnostics and figuring out what's not working and perhaps why as well so here for example let's go back to this catalog here if I go back to diagnostics there's a feedback here that 19 percent of my ad to carts cannot be retargeted what that means here is 47 of the items that were added to cart on this particular date were not matched back to my catalog so that means there's an item or 47 items on this particular website that someone had actually visited the product added to cart it had a legitimate ad id on it but the product was missing from my catalog that's a problem and let me just illustrate to you how so I've gone into the diagnostics you can access it through event data sources here as well and so what this shows me is my match rates over the last 30 days so generally Facebook say if it's above kind of 90 percent is a good match rate really you want to be aiming for 95 plus on a few days here it's dropped now why does it drop it could be that new products were added to the website but the catalog wasn't updated on time or it could be that the catalog and the website had a mismatch with IDs but the problem here is so for example here took a massive dip on the 19th about 40 percent of products or added to cart could not be retargeted in ads to customers using dynamic product ads which could have had a massive impact on performance so this is something you should check in on on a weekly basis I run an agency I run my own e-com stores I make sure that on a weekly basis I'm doing regular routine maintenance and checking my dynamic product catalog is one of those and making sure that things are working well you can also check purchases as well as views this one means that for those that were purchased so items purchased how many were matched back to catalog it would generally be a kind of higher number because especially where a retargeting hasn't kicked in and it hasn't transferred to purchase then it could be that those items that were not missed that were not matched were also not purchased and there could be different reasons for that you can kind of check on views as well so definitely here something on the 19th went strangely wrong and you look at sales didn't seem like the ones that were purchased were massively affected but that that would be cause for concern so that's just something to kind of keep an eye on for this particular one they were just missing items in the catalog that weren't updated from the site but it starts with understanding that there is a problem then you can analyze as well and then

## [15:14 - 17:05]

you can go down and see which particular URLs were affected and you can open those in a new tab and you can see the actual page check the pixel ID and figure out what's going on and check that with what's in your catalog you can also download that into a csv and kind of get maybe send that to a developer or someone else in your team for example so that's by URLs you can look at it by content ID this this one's a concern if there's a null ID it means no ID was passed and it could be that products on your e-com store and for some reason the the product ID is missing or it's not being passed back to the pixel so there's just a few things to keep an eye on and honestly this does have an impact on performance if you rely on dynamic product ads for your retargeting this will absolutely impact your performance so that's why I just want to stress to you how important it is to come in into your event date sources and analyze any problems with your catalog so there are just a few things to keep an eye on when it comes to the catalog this is just another catalog where we've been having problems trying to match products this is a clients catalog and they're actually running an e-com platform called big commerce I don't know if you use big commerce but it's really painful at times to get working not to say it's a bad platform but certainly the feed side of things has been a lot more difficult so it's really a case of keeping up to speed on what's working what's not working and making sure you're getting as many matches back as possible so that should give you a top line view of dynamic product ads how they work the diagnostics the tracking creating product sets viewing the products that are there maybe doing some editing etc and in subsequent videos you'll be able to see how I use dynamic product ads to make my account work more effectively
